entry.title: Re: Manage Teams and Responsibilities - Export and Import
entry.content:
Good day @EricAlo 

Thank you for your question

The reason you do not see “Manage Import” in your test tenant is because the help page you linked in your question is for BTP Responsibility Management service and not the segment for RESPONSIBILITY_MANAGEMENT_SCP. The Manage imports button exists in that service but it requires you to have the BusinessProcessSpecialistResponsibilityManagement BTP role. Basically, it also assumes you have maintained the same team setup in BTP and public cloud.

In public cloud, you cannot use JSON import to move Responsibility Management content inside the app. You can do this with Export Software Collection and Import Software Collection. So, there is no missing button in TEST for cloud. You are basically looking at the wrong documentation for your scenario. See Transport Responsibility Contexts, Rules, and Team Categories for more details.

When you use workflows, importing cannot create or map teams because your target tenant will not have some prerequisites for that. It is common that some users might be missing in your target system and therefore hinders the team mapping during import. See KBA 3625745 for some details.

Let me know if you have further questions.

 

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Populate the BP Address Comment using API
entry.content:
Dear @JastinC1,

In Public Cloud you can only write what the released OData service exposes in its $metadata. The Comments field you highlighted in the BP Address is not exposed in API_BUSINESS_PARTNER, so you can’t populate that standard field via this API.

What you can do instead, clean-core:

Option A: Use a custom field and expose it in the API (recommended)

If your goal is store a note/comment coming from an external system create your own field and send it via the same API:

  1. Custom Fields app
  2. Create a field in the Business Context related to Business Partner Address (text field)
  3. Enable Usage for OData Service = API_BUSINESS_PARTNER, so it becomes available in the payload
  4. Publish
  5. Populate the generated field your POST/PATCH to A_BusinessPartnerAddress

This won’t fill the standard Comments field, but it gives you an API-writeable comment that can also be placed on the UI and used in forms if needed

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_EXTENSIBILITY_EXPLORER/7dde0e0e3a294f01a6f7870731c5e4ad/d47e2418e4db45509b6111586842caf5.html

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/add-custom-fields-to-sap-s-4hana-business-partner-odata-api-entities/ba-p/13333784

Option B: Request SAP to enhance the API, only way to fill the standard field

If you must fill that standard Comments field (not a custom one), the only route in Public Cloud is an enhancement request via SAP influence/support because you can’t use unreleased in Public Cloud

More details below:

https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Loss Handling in the Consolidation under Equity method
entry.content:
Good day @Srinivas_Thatik 

Thank you for your question.

The IFRS compliant behaviour is to ensure you do not reduce your investment below zero and track unrecognised losses.

In public cloud, for your equity, the standard way to handle Group reporting is to use the statistical negative investment FS item. In “Specify Items for Equity Method Postings” you can configure statistical negative investment FS item. See KBA 3456831 and 3454699 for more details.

You can do that by opening your Manage Your Solution.

  • Navigate to S4 for Group Reporting à Consolidation of Investments à Specify items for Equity Methods Postings.
  • In “at equity scope”, ensure you have an FS item for Investment at Equity, for Equity Income or Loss and for Negative Investment that is used to hold unrecognised losses once the investment reaches zero. This is described in KBA 3456831 and 3454699.

That process will trigger the following:

  • It would reduce the investment down to zero. Any excess loss will be captured on the statistical negative investment FS item.
  • The system first offsets the statistical negative investment (your previously unrecognised losses). The investment balance will only be increased after that. See KBA 3456831 and 3454699 for more insights.

Let me know if you have further questions.

 

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Can the decision buttons be disabled in a to-do list...
entry.content:
Dear @sr_absoft,

In Public Cloud there’s no supported standard setting to centrally remove/disable the Approve / Reject buttons that appear directly on My Home -> To-Dos task cards or on Fiori notifications. Those buttons are quick actions that come from the workflow tasks decision options and SAP designed them specifically so users can act without opening the full object

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Can PR Overall Release enforce per-item approval?
entry.content:
Good day @kienngo 

Thank you for your question

The public cloud does not offer any support option to enforce different approvers per PR item and require all item specific approvals before the PR is released. It is because Overall Release happens at the header level. The system creates one workflow approval for the whole PR, so it cannot reliably route approvals and enforce completion for each item based on their cost center. This is described in KBA 3647061.

The option i suggest is to Use the workflow scenario Release of Purchase Requisition item so that:

  • Item 10 triggers approval to B or C
  • Item 20 triggers approval to E or F
  • The PR is effectively released only after all relevant items are approved (each item has its own workflow task). See Flexible Workflow for Purchase Requisitions for more insights.

Alternatively, you can consider keeping overall Release but accept a compromise. Which means you can only route the whole PR to a combined approver set (for example union of B/C and E/F) and approve sequentially, but you cannot guarantee B/C approves only item 10 and E/F approves only item 20 in a supported standard way with Overall Release.

Another option, which is a workaround would be to split the PR by cost center so approvals are naturally separated. This also mentioned in KBA 3647061.

Here is a quick guide on how to activate it:

Open Manage Workflows for PR

Choose the Release of Purchase Requisition Item

Define step condition and agent determination (cost center responsibility, teams, etc.)

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

 

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Discrepancy: PAL consumption quantity is 0 despite correct confirmation in Batch BOP - S/4HANA 2023
entry.content:
Hello experts,

I am encountering a critical discrepancy in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2023 FPS03 where "Schedule BOP Run" (Batch) grants the correct confirmed quantity but fails to record the Product Allocation (PAL) consumption.

Scenario:

Material: Relevant for aATP and PAL, Period Type: Day.

PAL Allocation: 100 units on Jan 31st, 100 units on Feb 1st.

PAL Sequence Settings:

Allow Past Periods: ON

Backward Consumption: 1

Forward Consumption: 0

Check Date Type: Material Availability Date (MAD).

Observed Problem:

In Jan: A customer orders 200 units. 100 units are confirmed against the Jan 31st quota, and 100 units remain as a backorder.

In Feb: I run a BOP (Strategy: Redistribute) via the "Schedule BOP Run" app.

Result: The backorder successfully receives a confirmed quantity of 100 units (Total 200 units confirmed).

The Issue: When checking the PAL monitor (e.g., "Monitor Product Allocation Periods"), the consumption quantity for this order shows 0 for both Jan 31st and Feb 1st. The confirmation is saved in the Sales Order, but PAL quotas are not reduced/charged.

Comparison: Using the "Manage Confirmations for Backorders" app (Interactive), the system works perfectly. Thanks to "Allow Past Periods = ON" and "Backward Consumption = 1", it correctly re-reads and allocates both Jan 31st and Feb 1st quotas, recording a total consumption of 200 units.

Questions:

Are there known cases in Batch BOP where the system updates the confirmed quantity in the business document but skips the update to PAL consumption buckets?

Does the batch execution engine handle the synchronization between accumulated backward consumption and database/liveCache updates differently than the interactive app when the MAD is in the past?

Since the interactive app (IBOP) handles the "Redistribute" strategy and backward consumption perfectly, could this be related to asynchronous update tasks or background processing constraints in 2023 FPS03?

Are there any specific SAP Notes regarding "Zero PAL consumption despite confirmation" for scheduled BOP runs?

Any insights or guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Best regards,

entry.title: Re: SAP Certification update: Practical exams roll out in 2026
entry.content:
Hello @DianaRoesner and SAP Certification Team,

I would like to take this opportunity to report a similar experience related to the new system-based practical certification exams, in the spirit of constructive feedback as the program evolves.

I completed the C_IEE2E – SAP Certified – Implementation Consultant – End-to-End Business Processes for SAP Business Suite (System-Based Practical Exam) on 25 January 2026. After submission, the exam status remained “Under Review” for several days, with the message advising not to start a new exam until the review was completed.

After contacting SAP Certification Support, I was informed that:

  • The certification is currently in a pilot phase

  • Detailed result explanations are not yet available

  • My exam attempt was closed and recredited

While I appreciate the recredit and fully support the shift toward performance-based, real-world certification, this outcome has left me slightly unsure about the recommended next step — whether to immediately retake the same practical exam or wait until some of the pilot-phase processes (such as scoring transparency and review timelines) are further stabilized.

I am sharing this here to:

  • Confirm whether other candidates have encountered similar review/closure situations

  • Understand whether this behavior is expected in certain edge cases during the pilot

  • Get guidance from SAP on best practices for candidates affected by this scenario

I remain very positive about the direction SAP Certification is taking and appreciate the openness to community feedback during this transition.

Thank you in advance for any clarification or guidance

entry.title: Why Document Like it's 1999
entry.content:
Your First Day on the New Project

You open the documentation repository. There's an Excel sheet titled “RICEFWInventoryv3FINAL2.xlsx”

You scan through:

  • Row 47: “Enhancement ZENHMAT001 – Material validation”
  • Row 89: “Report ZMMSTOCK013 – Stock overview”
    Row 134: “Form ZSDORDERPRINT – Order output”
  • Row 201: “Interface ZIFPRICING002 – Pricing calculation”

Your task: “Fix the credit check bug in sales order creation.”

You stare at the screen. Which row is that?

You search for “credit”. Eight matches. Three are reports. Two are enhancements. One is a workflow you've never heard of.

You ask your colleague: “Where's the credit check logic?”

She sighs. “Check the validation BADI in ZFICO_CUST. Or is it the user exit? Actually, I think there's also a workflow step... let me check my notes from six months ago...”

Three hours later, you still haven't found it.

Sound familiar?

The Problem with RICEFW Documentation

RICEFW Thinks in Technical Artifacts

RICEFW (Reports, Interfaces, Conversions, Enhancements, Forms, Workflows) was invented when SAP development meant: – Modifying a monolithic ECC system – Creating standalone reports – Adding enhancements to standard transactions – Building separate interfaces

Documentation followed the same pattern: categorize by technical artifact type.

This made sense in 1999.

RAP Thinks in Business Capabilities

Fast-forward to 2026. You're building a Sales Order business object in RAP:

ZI_SALESORDER_M (Business Object)
├── Data Model (CDS views)
│   ├── ZI_SALESORDER_M (interface view)
│   └── ZC_SALESORDER_M (projection view)
├── Behavior Definition
│   ├── Validations (validateCustomer, validateCredit)
│   ├── Determinations (calculatePrice)
│   └── Actions (releaseOrder, cancelOrder)
├── Business Events
│   └── OrderCreated, OrderReleased
└── Service
    └── ZUI_SALESORDER_O4 (OData V4)

Everything about Sales Orders in one cohesive business object.

Yet your documentation still says: – “Check Enhancement ZENHSD001” – “See Report ZSDORDERLIST” – “Refer to Form ZSDPRINTORDER”

The disconnect is killing you.

What RAP Actually Gave Us - besides RAP itself

Here's what's often missed in RAP discussions:

RAP isn't just a new technical framework. It's SAP finally embracing domain-driven design principles that the rest of the software world has used for years.

  • Business objects that model business capabilities
  • Clean separation of concerns
  • Event-driven architecture
  • Clear service boundaries

This is how modern software should be built.

And when you build this way, your documentation can finally match how people hopefully think.

Not “Which enhancement modifies field X?”

But “How does the Sales Order capability work?”

RAP enables the documentation style we should have been using all along.

Now the good stuff - Three Documentation Layers for RAP

Layer 1: Business Object Canvas

The idea: One wiki page per RAP business object. Everything about that capability in one place.

Here's what it actually looks like:

Business Object: Sales Order

Business Capability: Complete sales order lifecycle from customer inquiry through order fulfillment

Location in System: – Package: ZRAP_SD_ORDER – Root Entity: ZI_SALESORDER_M – Projection: ZC_SALESORDER_M – Service Binding: ZUI_SALESORDER_O4 (OData V4, Published) – Fiori App: Sales Order Management – Main Transport: ZTRK900123

Team & Ownership: – Development Team: Retail Sales Team – Product Owner: Jane Smith – Technical Lead: John Doe – Last Major Update: 2025-01-27

Behavior & Operations:

Standard Operations: – Create, Update, Delete (all enabled) – Draft handling: Enabled – ETag field: LastChangedAt

Validations (run on save):

  • validateCustomer Checks customer exists and is not blocked
  • validateCreditLimit – Verifies customer credit limit not exceeded
  • validateMaterial – Ensures material exists and valid for sales
  • validateDeliveryDate – Must be in the future

Determinations (auto-calculations):

  • calculateNetAmount – Runs when quantity or price changes
  • setDefaultPaymentTerms – Runs on create (from customer master)
  • determineDeliveryDate – Calculates based on material availability

Actions (user-triggered):

  • releaseOrder – Releases order to warehouse (publishes OrderReleased event)
  • cancelOrder – Cancels entire order (publishes OrderCancelled event)
  • splitOrderItem – Splits line item into multiple delivery schedules

Events & Integration:

Events Published:

  • OrderCreated – When order activated from draft – Subscribers: Inventory BO (reserves stock), Analytics Service
  • OrderReleased – When release action executed – Subscribers: Warehouse BO (starts picking), Billing BO (creates invoices
  • OrderCancelled – When cancel action executed – Subscribers: Inventory BO (releases reservation)

Events Subscribed:

  • StockReserved (from Inventory BO) – Updates order status to “Ready for Release”
  • StockShortage (from Inventory BO) – Marks order as “Partially Allocated”

Dependencies:

Upstream (What We Consume):

Material Master (I_Product) – Owner: Master Data Team – Access: Read-only via association – Fields used: Product, ProductType, BaseUnit, SalesText – Impact if unavailable: Order creation blocked (validation fails) – Contact: master-data-team@company.com

Customer Master (I_Customer

  • Owner: Master Data Team
  • Access: Read-only via association
  • Fields used: Customer, CustomerName, CreditLimit, PaymentTerms
  • Impact if unavailable: Order creation blocked (validation fails)
  • Contact: master-data-team@company.com

Pricing Engine (ZZPRICEENGINE – Function Module)

  • Owner: Pricing Team – Access: Synchronous RFC call (wrapped in ZCLPRICING_ACL)
  • Called when: During determination (calculateNetAmount)
  • Timeout: 5 seconds
  • Impact if unavailable: Order creation blocked, error shown to user
  • Why synchronous: [See ADR-018]
  • Contact: pricing-team@company.com

Downstream (Who Consumes Us): 

  • Inventory BO (subscribes to OrderCreated, OrderCancelled)
  • Warehouse BO (subscribes to OrderReleased)
  • Billing BO (reads order data via association, subscribes to OrderReleased)
  • Analytics Dashboard (reads via OData for reporting)
  • Mobile Sales App (reads/writes via OData)

Key Architecture Decisions: 

  • [ADR-015] Why we chose managed RAP implementation  
  • [ADR-018] Why pricing happens in determinations (not actions)
  • [ADR-023] How we handle external pricing service integration
  •  [ADR-032] Why credit check is synchronous (blocks save)

Related Documentation: 

  • API Documentation: OData Service Spec 
  • Process Documentation: Order-to-Cash Process (coming in Part 2 of this series) 
  • Test Data: Customer 1000001, Material MAT-TEST-001

That's the canvas. One page. ~5 minutes to scan. Everything about Sales Orders.

Compare this to hunting through a RICEFW spreadsheet with 200 rows.

Layer 2: Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)

The hard part isn't writing RAP code. It's deciding why to write it a certain way, and later remembering why that decision was taken.

The Industry Solution: Architecture Decision Records

This problem isn't unique to SAP or RAP. It's why organizations like AWS and IBM actively recommend using Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) to capture the reasoning behind technical decisions.

AWS Prescriptive Guidance recommends using architectural decision records to streamline technical decision-making for a software development project. Architectural Decision Records IBM has documented their use of ADRs in production systems, showing how they help teams maintain context over time.

The principle is simple: when you make a significant technical decision, document why you made it, what alternatives you considered, and what consequences you accepted.

Six months later, when someone asks "Why?", you point them to the ADR.

Example ADR:

ADR-018: Calculate Pricing in Determinations (Not Actions)

Date: 2025-01-15
Status: ✅ Accepted
Deciders: Tech Lead, Solution Architect, Senior Developer

Context:

We need to calculate item pricing in Sales Order BO. Two options:

Option A: Determination (automatic) – Runs automatically during save sequence – User sees price immediately when quantity changes – Can't be skipped

Option B: Action (manual) – User must click “Calculate Price” button – More control over when calculation happens – Can be skipped if needed

Current situation: – Pricing engine (ZZPRICE_ENGINE) is legacy FM, ~2 second response time – Business requires instant price feedback for sales reps – Complex pricing rules (discounts, surcharges, customer agreements)

Decision:

Use determination to calculate pricing automatically on quantity/material changes.

Rationale:

Why determination:

  • Sales reps need instant price visibility
  • Prevents orders being saved with wrong/missing prices
  • Aligns with UX requirement: “price updates as you type”
  • Consistent with standard SAP behavior

Why not action: 

  • Extra click burdens sales reps (average 50 orders/day/rep)
  • Risk of forgetting to calculate = wrong invoices
  • User testing showed confusion with manual pricing button

Risks accepted: 

  • 2-second save time (acceptable per UX testing)
  • Pricing service must be highly available (99.9% SLA)

Consequences:

Positive: 

  • Instant price feedback for sales reps
  • No risk of missing price calculation
  • Reduced training time (automatic behavior)
  • Consistent UX across all sales channels

Negative: 

  • Every save calls pricing engine (load concern)
  • Can't skip pricing for “draft” scenarios
  • Pricing service downtime blocks all orders

Mitigations: 

  • Pricing service has 5-second timeout (fail-fast)
  • Fallback: save order with “Price Pending” flag, batch re-price later
  • Pricing team committed to 99.9% SLA
  • Monitoring alert if pricing calls >3 seconds

Implementation Notes:

determination calculateNetAmount on modify {
  field Quantity;
  field Material;
  field Customer;
}

In behavior implementation: – Call ZCLPRICINGACL wrapper (not direct FM call) – Wrapper implements circuit breaker pattern – Logs all pricing calls for debugging

Related: – [ADR-023] Anti-corruption layer for pricing service – [ADR-040] Pricing service timeout handling

The principle: Six months from now, when someone asks “Why does save take 2 seconds?”, you point to ADR-018. Everything is documented.

The principle: When Master Data announces “We're changing Material descriptions from 40 to 80 characters,” you know instantly that 12 BOs need coordination.

Part 4: Why This Works

RICEFW Docs BO Canvas Approach
ZTRK900123 – Enhancement for credit checkSales Order BO → validateCreditLimit
5 spreadsheet tabs1 wiki page
Technical artifact typesBusiness capabilities
“Why?” is in meeting notes“Why?” is in ADRs
Dependencies invisibleDependency matrix
3 hours to find credit check3 minutes to find credit check

Real impact:

Onboarding time: 

  • RICEFW:  weeks to understand one domain
  • BO Canvas: days to understand one domain

Incident response: 

  • RICEFW: “Where's the logic?” →  hours–
  • BO Canvas: “Check Sales Order BO canvas” → minutes

Impact analysis: 

  • RICEFW: “Who uses Material Master?” → “¯\(ツ)/¯”
  • BO Canvas: “Check dependency matrix” → 12 BOs listed

Part 5: What This Doesn't Solve (Yet)

You've documented your Sales Order BO beautifully. Dependencies are clear. Decisions are captured.

Then someone asks: “Walk me through what happens when a customer creates an order. Step by step. Including credit check, stock reservation, pricing, and warehouse handoff.”

You point them to four BO Canvases:

  • Sales Order BO
  • Credit Check BO
  • Inventory BO
    Shipping BO

They read all four. They check dependencies. They still don't understand the sequence of events , what happens if credit check fails etc.

Business Object Canvases document capabilities, ADRs document decisions, Dependency matrices document connections.

But none of them document the actual business process flow, ant that's why that's the topic for the next post in this series. 

Next in this series: Part 2: Documenting Business Processes – From BOs to Flows

What's your biggest documentation challenge? Drop a comment below.

entry.title: Re: No cost center mandatory in the app Post goods issue...
entry.content:
Good day @Kristina06 

Thank you for your questions.

Question 1: How can I find where this is maintained?

It is not maintained as a mandatory field setting for MIGO. The app uses a different posting interface and a fixed process design:

  • The app posts an unplanned goods issue for scrapping, and the stock is written off with 551 in inventory management. See Post Goods Issue - Unplanned and Feature Comparison for Posting Unplanned Goods Issue for more details.
  • The system does not require assignment object like a cost center for the scrapping process. It automatically derives it through the scrap expense account and profit center.

This is to say, there is no SSCUI where you can turn off cost center requirement.

Question 2: Why does the app not require the cost center even when its mandatory for the movement type 551 in MIGO?

In Post Goods Issue – Unplanned, the scrapping process is designed to be posted without a cost center. If your business requires a cost center, that is a different process. You should post a consumption goods issue with account assignment, not scrapping.

Question 3: How does the app know which movement type to use for scrapping?

For unplanned goods issue, the movement type is determined by Scrapping, Sampling or Consumption. So it depends on the process you choose. For Scrapping, it posts with movement type 551 in the connected IM system. The public cloud restricts changing this standard mapping. See Posting Unplanned Goods Issue

To summarise:

  • If you truly need a cost center on the posting, use a goods issue to cost center process through the relevant IM app, or use an unplanned goods issue option that allows you to assign account e.g., cost center, order or WBS instead of the scrapping process.
  • If you only need to report within your organisation, you can rely on the profit center that is automatically derived or adjust your reporting logic accordingly.

Let me know if you have further questions.

 

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Change or Diable Timesheet bell icon notification for...
entry.content:
Dear @Meenu_Wavie,

From your screenshot, this is an SAP Fiori launchpad in-app notification (bell icon) that gets created when a time entry is rejected. In Public Cloud there are two important points:

First:

For standard time entry rejection notifications the wording is delivered by SAP and not meant to be customer-editable in Public Cloud. SAP documents this capability as work with notifications for rejected time entries view and navigate from the notification but it does not describe a supported option to rewrite the text

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/doc/7c9e0bbbd1664c2581b2038a1c7ae4b3/2602.500/en-US/FSD_CE2602.pdf

Second:

If your notification is coming from Situation Handling, then the text can be adapted in the Manage Situation Types app because Situation Handling supports maintaining in-app message text / notification title

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/516c8ecb7462453ca430a42481cc33ec.html

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/situation-handling-how-to-configure-and-trigger-situations/ba-p/12855788

In that case, you would:

  • Open Manage Situation Types or Manage Situation Types Extended
  • Find the relevant situation type/template for rejected time entries
  • Adjust the In-App Situation Message / Notification Title fields depending on framework
  • Save/activate

If we can’t change it can we stop this notification for all users?

Option A: Per-user suppression is supported

Each user can switch off a notification type in:
User Actions (avatar) -> Settings -> Notifications
There you can disable specific notification types or notifications in general, depending on what your system exposes

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/btp/sap-fiori-launchpad-for-sap-btp/setting-notification-preferences

Option B: Global turn it off for everyone is not available for standard notifications in Public Cloud

For the standard timesheet rejection bell notification, there isn’t a typical Public Cloud configuration switch to centrally suppress only that one message for all users while keeping approval/rejection active

Option C: Global suppression is possible if it’s Situation Handling

If the rejected time entry message is driven by Situation Handling, then you can stop it centrally by disabling notifications at the situation type level for example, disabling the situation type or send notifications depending on the framework/version

More details below:

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/situation-handling-how-to-configure-and-trigger-situations/ba-p/12855788

Option D Email notifications

Separately from the bell icon, SAP also provides jobs/templates to send email notifications for rejected time entries. If your users are getting emails too, you stop that by not scheduling / disabling that job

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c56f622a2edf491b9f1b596b55587009/b710108518854368b59b0d8212db3f6d.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0bebd08dffca45afa67b1f751199afd0/22d8f19f8c2d4a84801629bb7e0eafbc.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Z* Custom CDS Views Not Visible in SAC Live Connectio...
entry.content: Hi, for live connection to SAC, you don't need to generate an odata service for custom CDS views. Therefore, @odata.publish:true annotation is not required. Kind Regards.

entry.title: Re: Z* Custom CDS Views Not Visible in SAC Live Connectio...
entry.content:
Hi,

For live connection from S/4HANA on premise or S/4HANA Cloud, @odata.publish:true is not required (only for import connection) for custom CDS views. Create another CDS view based on the standard CDS view and remove the @odata.publish:true, release the CDS view (C1-released using ABAP Development Tools to switch the API-state visibility -> properties of the CDS view) and check again with this new custom CDS view. You may find the first custom CDS view that you have created with @odata. publish:true listed accessing S/4HANA PCE through an import connection.,

Hope this helps.

Kind Regards.

entry.title: SAP and the Mercedes-Accelerating Innovation Beyond the Track
entry.content:
SAP is one of the world’s leading enterprise software companies, recognized for its solutions in ERP, data analytics, artificial intelligence and cloud computing. Its partnership with the Mercedes-AMG PETRONAS Formula One Team, one of the most successful teams in Formula 1 history, demonstrates how enterprise technology can drive performance not only on the racetrack, but across the entire organization.

In Formula 1, where milliseconds define winners and regulations are increasingly strict, data, governance and agility have become as critical as engine power and aerodynamics.

1. SAP’s Role Within the Mercedes-AMG PETRONAS F1 Team

SAP is a strategic technology partner to Mercedes-AMG PETRONAS, supporting the team’s digital core and operational backbone.

SAP Cloud ERP and SAP S/4HANA Cloud

These platforms serve as the team’s central digital system, integrating: finance and controlling; procurement and supply chain; inventory and asset management; operational planning and reporting.

With thousands of components per car and extremely tight development cycles, having a single, real-time source of truth is essential.

SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and SAP Business AI

Through SAP BTP and embedded AI capabilities, Mercedes is able to build custom applications for racing operations and logistics; automate workflows across departments; use advanced analytics and predictive models for planning and decision-making; connect technical, operational and financial data in a secure cloud environment.

This allows the team to operate with the same precision off the track that it demands on it.

2. Innovation, Information Security and Process Agility

Data-Driven Innovation

Formula 1 teams generate massive volumes of data every race weekend. SAP solutions enable Mercedes to transform this data into actionable insights, supporting:

  • scenario simulations for operational and financial planning;

  • optimization of supply chain and resource allocation;

  • faster reaction to regulatory and competitive changes;

  • continuous innovation within strict cost and time constraints.

Decisions that once relied heavily on experience and intuition are now reinforced by real-time analytics and AI-driven insights.

Information Security in a High-Stakes Environment

In Formula 1, data is intellectual property — and a competitive advantage. SAP cloud solutions provide:

  • robust access control and role-based authorizations;

  • encrypted data storage and secure cloud environments;

  • audit trails and traceability for sensitive financial and operational data;

  • high availability during critical periods such as race weekends.

This level of security protects both strategic racing data and financial information, ensuring trust, compliance and resilience.

Agility becomes a competitive advantage — not just in racing strategy, but in how the entire team operates.

3. Audit, Compliance and Predictive Performance Analytics

Audit and Regulatory Compliance

Since the introduction of the FIA cost cap, financial transparency and auditability have become mission-critical.

SAP enables Mercedes to monitor costs in near real time; ensure full traceability of expenses across development, manufacturing and racing operations; generate audit-ready financial reports aligned with FIA regulations; enforce segregation of duties and internal controls.

This minimizes regulatory risk and allows leadership to make confident decisions based on trusted, auditable data.

Predictive Analytics Applied to Car Performance

Beyond finance, SAP plays a key role in predictive performance analysis. By combining telemetry, historical race data, simulations and operational data, the team can:

  • predict component wear and potential failures;

  • optimize race setups and strategic decisions;

  • anticipate operational and logistical impacts of technical changes;

  • link technical performance with cost and resource implications.

The result is a proactive approach: acting before problems occur, rather than reacting after performance is lost.

4. Mercedes-AMG PETRONAS Drivers and Formula 1 Season 2026

Mercedes Drivers for 2026

For the 2026 Formula 1 season, Mercedes-AMG PETRONAS will be represented by:

  • George Russell

  • Andrea Kimi Antonelli

This driver lineup combines experience with emerging talent, aligned with the team’s long-term performance strategy.

Conclusion: From Formula 1 to the Enterprise World

The partnership between SAP and the Mercedes-AMG PETRONAS Formula One Team proves that success at the highest level depends on more than speed.

Through advanced analytics, predictive intelligence, rigorous auditability, information security and process agility, SAP helps Mercedes compete at the limit — efficiently, securely and within the rules.

If SAP helps accelerate Formula 1, imagine what it can do for your company.

Sources and References

This article is based on information from the following reliable and official sources:

  • SAP Newsroom (Global, Brazil and Portugal) – official announcements and case studies on the SAP–Mercedes partnership

  • SAP SE – documentation on SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP BTP, SAP Business AI, analytics and audit capabilities

  • Mercedes-AMG PETRONAS Formula One Team – publicly available information on operations and technology strategy

  • Formula1.com and FIA publications – official regulations, cost cap framework and 2026 calendar details

  • Specialized motorsport media (including CNN Brasil) – confirmation of drivers and team structures for 2026

entry.title: SAP Databricks - Notebook supported Programming languages
entry.content:
What “SAP Databricks” means for notebooks

SAP Databricks is a Databricks edition integrated with SAP Business Data Cloud/Datasphere for governed SAP data products, sharing, and serverless access. Its user‑guide pages emphasize Python & SQL notebooks, built‑in visualizations, and serverless compute within the SAP context. SAP Databricks tenants, speak primarily about Python + SQL, Scala and R.

From the SAP Databricks docs: “Databricks notebooks in SAP Databricks support Python and SQL, and allow users to embed visualizations alongside links, images, and commentary written in markdown.”

Supported languages (and how they’re used)

1) Python

  • Best for: data wrangling with PySpark, ML (scikit‑learn, MLflow), plotting, notebooks with visualizations and debugging.
  • Notebook specifics: SAP Databricks exposes an interactive Python debugger and notebook‑scoped package management (via %pip).
  • General Databricks guidance: Python is a top recommendation for new projects.
# PySpark DataFrame example
df = spark.table("sap_data.cashflow.cashflowforecast")  # Example mounted SAP catalog path
df.groupBy("CompanyCode").agg({"Amount":"sum"}).show(20)

2) SQL

  • Best for: ad‑hoc analytics, BI‑style queries, quick aggregations, and powering built‑in notebook visualizations.
  • Where it runs: against your lakehouse tables (Unity Catalog) and Serverless SQL warehouses for low‑friction, governed access.
-- Query SAP data product mounted in Unity Catalog
SELECT CompanyCode, SUM(Amount) AS TotalAmount
FROM sap_data.cashflow.cashflowforecast
GROUP BY CompanyCode
ORDER BY TotalAmount DESC
``

3) Markdown (for narrative + visuals)

  • Purpose: structure your notebook/blog‑style narrative, add images/links, and keep analysis reproducible and readable.
  • Status: available in notebooks for inline documentation in both SAP Databricks and the general platform.
%md
### Cash Flow by Company Code
This section analyzes forecasted cash flow from SAP data products mounted in Unity Catalog.
``

Package management in notebooks

  • Use %pip install <package> to install Python libraries at notebook scope, improving reproducibility and avoiding cluster‑wide changes. This is supported on Databricks runtimes and is commonly documented for notebook productivity.
%pip install prophet
from prophet import Prophet

Visualizations

  • Both SQL and Python cells can produce tabular outputs that you can convert into built‑in charts directly from the result UI—handy for executive readouts without exporting to separate BI tools.

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-databricks-plotly-a-deadly-match-for-analytics-excellence/ba-p/14247815 

Serverless compute notes (useful context for your readers)

  • SAP Databricks encourages serverless for both notebooks and SQL, which simplifies cluster management and speeds up ad‑hoc work. Great to highlight for analysts and data scientists who don’t want to babysit clusters.

entry.title: Re: The consequences of implementing strictly Shift-left architectures
entry.content:
Hello @MKreitlein , I haven't found time to respond earlier, sorry for that. You were asking for the "beef", but I'm not fully sure what you were looking for.

Why did I write this article? It's the result of a "thought experiment" (with relation to a practical case I'm currently working on) that deals with what would happen if one wants to implement strictly a "Shift-left" approach. It is not product specific assumption. The consequences apply almost equally, if you use any other data platform as a receiver. The impact is more organizational than technical. So I summed this up. As my origin is SAP Analytics, of course I have a good understanding what SAP offered in the past and what it is offering currently. To get more "beef" for implementing shift-left on different platform combinations, a whole book can be written, I guess. 

Hope this helps, have a great Sunday. Phil

entry.title: Re: The consequences of implementing strictly Shift-left architectures
entry.content: Hi @mariodefe , thank you for your time. Yes, I agree fully with you. The impact on organization, mind set and skills of teams is much bigger than one could suspect initially, at least in my case. That we "loose" a level in the medallion archtecture is no break with the architecture as such, it just remains empty. In all cases, sooner or later, more flexible approaches will be implemented without invalidating the purpose of the three existing layers, as we see it already emerging. But that is another discussion. Best wishes for a happy Sunday from a rainy Madrid. Phil

entry.title: SAP Databricks : Query History [ Deep Dive Into Its Features, Benefits, and Practical Usefulness ]
entry.content:
What is the Databricks Query History Page?

The Query History interface provides a centralized, chronological record of SQL queries executed within a workspace. It includes:

  • Query text
  • Start time
  • Duration
  • Compute resource used (SQL Warehouse)
  • Source (notebook, editor, dashboard, API)
  • Execution status
  • User who executed the query

In the screenshot, we see a list of repeated SELECT statements running against various tables (e.g., cs_title, cs_plan, cdl_job), executed on a Serverless Starter Warehouse over the last 7 days.

This interface is accessible within the SQL > Query History section of the Databricks UI.

Key Features

1. Filter Panel

The panel allows filtering by:

  • User
  • Time range (Last 7 days, 24h, custom)
  • Source (Notebooks, Dashboards, Editor, etc.)
  • SQL Warehouse
  • Statement ID
  • Status (Succeeded, Failed)
  • Duration

This granular filtering is crucial for narrowing down specific operations.

2. Chronologically Organized Query Log

Each row displays:

  • Query text preview
  • Execution timestamp
  • Duration in milliseconds
  • Source of execution
  • Warehouse used
  • User who executed it

This makes it easy to scan tens or hundreds of queries quickly.

3. Query Detail Drill‑down

Each query links to a full execution page that shows:

  • Execution plan
  • Query profile
  • SQL text
  • Query output
  • Error details (if any)

This transforms the page into a debugging powerhouse.

4. Serverless SQL Warehouse Information

The screenshot shows all queries running on a Serverless Starter Warehouse, which highlights:

  • Auto-scaling compute
  • Zero management overhead
  • Pay-as-you-go query execution

Pairing Serverless SQL with Query History magnifies its effectiveness. 

Why Databricks Query History is Extremely Useful

1. Debugging and Troubleshooting Becomes Effortless

When a query takes unexpectedly long or fails:

  • You can instantly see its duration.
  • You can open the detail page for execution plans.
  • You can compare it against previous runs.

This dramatically reduces the time needed to identify performance bottlenecks.

2. Enhances Team Collaboration and Transparency

In collaborative analytics environments, multiple engineers or analysts may operate on the same data:

  • Query History helps teams review who ran what.
  • It prevents duplication of work.
  • It creates visibility into ongoing analytical processes.
  • It supports knowledge sharing (e.g., reusing helpful queries).

Even if teammates are in different time zones or working asynchronously, Query History becomes a shared logbook.

3. Supports Compliance and Auditability

Organizations in finance, healthcare, manufacturing, and other regulated industries require traceability of data access.

Query History provides:

  • A permanent audit trail
  • User-level accountability
  • SQL-level visibility into data operations

This strengthens governance and compliance.

4. Helps Optimize SQL Warehouses and Reduce Costs

By analyzing query durations and compute usage, teams can:

  • Identify expensive long-running queries
  • Tune SQL for better performance
  • Right-size or auto-scale warehouses
  • Avoid unnecessary compute costs

For serverless warehouses especially, faster queries translate directly into cost savings.

5. Speeds Up Development and Prototyping

Data engineers and analysts can:

  • Revisit previously executed queries
  • Copy, reuse, or refine earlier logic
  • Track their experimentation steps

This accelerates SQL development cycles and improves productivity.

6. Useful for Training and Onboarding

New team members can:

  • Explore past queries for examples
  • Learn company-approved SQL patterns
  • Understand how datasets are used in real workflows

The Query History effectively becomes a self-updating knowledge base.

 

Conclusion: A Small UI Panel with Big Impact

The Databricks Query History page—shown in above screenshot—may look like a list of simple SQL statements, but it’s much more than that. It’s a foundational feature that boosts team productivity, enhances transparency, improves governance, diagnoses performance issues, and helps control compute cost.

Whether you’re a data engineer optimizing pipelines, an analyst exploring customer tables, or an architect monitoring compliance—Query History provides the clarity and traceability you need.

By combining this feature with Serverless SQL Warehouses, organizations unlock a powerful, efficient, and hassle‑free analytics experience.

entry.title: SAP Databricks CLI: Unlocking Automation and Efficiency in Data Engineering
entry.content:
The Databricks CLI (Command-Line Interface) is a powerful, open-source tool that enables users to interact with the Databricks platform directly from the terminal, command prompt, or automation scripts. In the context of SAP Databricks — the natively embedded, SAP-managed version of Databricks within SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) — the CLI provides the same core functionality as in standard Databricks deployments on AWS, Azure, or GCP.
 
While SAP Databricks focuses on seamless integration with SAP data (via zero-copy Delta Sharing, semantic preservation, and tight coupling with SAP applications like S/4HANA), the CLI remains the standard Databricks CLI (version 0.205+ as of 2026, in public preview for many advanced features). It allows data engineers, administrators, and developers to manage workspaces, automate workflows, and handle SAP-enriched data pipelines more efficiently outside the web UI.

What Is the Databricks CLI?

 
The Databricks CLI wraps the Databricks REST API, giving you command-line access to almost every resource and operation available in the platform. Key capabilities include:
  • Managing compute clusters
  • Handling jobs and workflows
  • Syncing code, notebooks, and files (DBFS or workspace)
  • Administering Unity Catalog (metastores, catalogs, shares)
  • Configuring account-level settings
In SAP Databricks environments, you can use it to:
  • Manage workspaces integrated with SAP BDC
  • Automate ingestion/transformation of SAP data products shared via Delta Sharing
  • Orchestrate jobs that combine SAP context-rich data with external sources
Installation and SetupInstallation is straightforward and identical to standard Databricks:
 
# On macOS (Homebrew)
brew install databricks

# Or download binary from GitHub releases
# https://github.com/databricks/cli/releases

# Then configure (OAuth or token-based)
databricks configure --profile sap-bdc
You can define multiple profiles in ~/.databrickscfg for different environments (e.g., dev SAP Databricks workspace vs. prod).

Useful Commands for Everyday and SAP-Relevant Work

Here are some of the most practical commands, especially valuable in SAP-integrated scenarios:
  1. Cluster Management
    • List clusters: databricks clusters list
    • Get cluster details: databricks clusters get <cluster-id>
    • Start/stop/restart: databricks clusters start <cluster-id>
    • Create from JSON config: databricks clusters create --json-file cluster.json
{
  "cluster_name": "prod-cluster",
  "spark_version": "13.3.x-scala2.12",
  "node_type_id": "i3.xlarge",
  "autotermination_minutes": 30,
  "enable_elastic_disk": true
}

→ Benefit in SAP contexts: Quickly spin up clusters for processing large SAP extractions or ML training on business data without clicking through the UI.

  • Jobs and Workflows
    • List jobs: databricks jobs list
    • Run job immediately: databricks jobs run-now --job-id <job-id>
    • List job runs: databricks jobs list-runs --job-id <job-id>
    • Export job config: databricks jobs get --job-id <job-id> --output-json > job.json
{
  "name": "daily-batch-job",
  "tasks": [
    {
      "task_key": "ingest-data",
      "job_cluster_key": "prod-cluster",
      "spark_python_task": {
        "python_file": "/Shared/ingest.py"
      }
    }
  ]
}

→ Automate nightly ETL jobs that transform SAP delta-shared tables into analytics-ready layers.

  • Workspace & Notebooks
    • Sync local folder workspace: databricks sync . /Users/your.name/project
    • List notebooks: databricks workspace ls /Users/your.name
    • Export notebook: databricks workspace export /path/to/notebook --format SOURCE > notebook.py
    → Ideal for CI/CD: push transformed notebooks or Delta Live Tables pipelines from Git → SAP Databricks workspace.

  • DBFS (Databricks File System)
    • Upload file: databricks fs cp localfile.txt dbfs:/mnt/sap-data/
    • Download: databricks fs cp dbfs:/output/results.csv .
    • List: databricks fs ls dbfs:/mnt/
    → Move SAP export CSVs, model artifacts, or interim results without browser-based uploads.

  • Unity Catalog (governance for SAP data)
    • List catalogs: databricks catalog list
    • List shares (Delta Sharing): databricks shares list
    • Create recipient for sharing to/from SAP BDC: (via API/CLI equivalents)
    → Manage access to SAP Business Data Cloud shared data products securely.

  • Other Handy Ones
    • Current version: databricks --version
    • Help for any group: databricks clusters --help
    • Output formatting: --output JSON or TABLE

Key Benefits of Using Databricks CLI (Especially with SAP Databricks)


Benefit
 
Description
 Value in SAP Databricks Context
Automation & Scripting
Script repetitive tasks in Bash, PowerShell, Python, etc.
Automate provisioning for SAP data pipelines or dev/test envs
CI/CD Integration
Native fit with GitHub Actions, Jenkins, Azure DevOps
Deploy notebooks/pipelines that process SAP + non-SAP data
Speed & Efficiency
No need to switch to browser; faster for power users
Quick troubleshooting of SAP Delta Sharing ingestion jobs
Bulk & Admin Operations
Create 50+ users, assign catalogs, manage IP lists in one script
Onboard teams working with SAP-enriched Unity Catalog assets
Error Reduction
Avoid manual UI mistakes in complex setups
Consistent handling of semantically rich SAP data products
Remote & Headless
Run from servers, containers, or local machines without UI access
Automate in restricted corporate environments
Cost Control
Script cluster start/stop based on schedule/usage
Optimize spend on clusters processing large SAP datasets
  • Explore Databricks CLI Reference
  • Combine CLI with Databricks Terraform Provider for infrastructure-as-code
  • Build end-to-end pipelines using Databricks Workflows + CLI scripts

Conclusion

 
The Databricks CLI transforms how you interact with SAP Databricks — turning a powerful but UI-heavy platform into something scriptable, repeatable, and DevOps-friendly. Whether you're an SAP data engineer building real-time analytics on S/4HANA extracts, a platform admin governing Delta Sharing between SAP BDC and other systems, or an AI developer training models on enriched business data, the CLI saves hours of manual work every week.If you're just starting with SAP Databricks, install the CLI early — it will quickly become your go-to for anything beyond quick exploratory work in the notebook editor.

entry.title: Characteristics-Based Planning: Shelf Life Meets Compliance
entry.content:
The Problem: When Systems Can't See What Matters

In pharmaceutical, food and beverage, and consumer goods industries, not all units are equal. Products cannot be any more treated as homogeneous SKUs. Batches have remaining shelf life. They carry regulatory approvals for specific markets. They age every day the planning system runs

In regulated industries, batches within the same SKU aren't interchangeable. A pharmaceutical batch with 60 days of remaining shelf life approved for US and EU markets is not the same as a batch with 90 days remaining approved only for EU. They cannot be treated equally in planning.

The result? Planners export data to spreadsheets, manually check approval matrices and expiration dates, then adjust plans accordingly. When demand changes or new batches arrive, the process repeats.

The consequences cascade differently across organizations:

Business leaders see the waste-compliance squeeze. Conservative planners avoid compliance risks by scrapping usable inventory. Aggressive planners maximize utilization but this can trigger audit findings

IT teams inherit the integration burden. ERP systems track batch expiry dates. Regulatory/Quality management systems hold approval records. Planning generates demand plans. Someone has to connect these further into feasible Supply!

What's Changed:

CBP with enhanced Shelf-Life planning is now available for SAP7F and Harmonized Planning Area (IBP2)!! 

Release 2602 marks a significant step forward. Characteristics-Based Planning (CBP) is now available on the Harmonized planning Area and we've fused the two critical dimensions: Flexibility of CBP and shelf life management --> unified with regulatory approval constraints.

With this here is what you get: 

Guaranteed Compliance: Regulatory + Quality(Shelf-Life) constraints evaluated simultaneously - no more choosing between fresh or compliant.

IBP now plans at the batch + CBP characteristic level together. It understands that Batch A has 60 days remaining shelf life and is approved for EU and US markets, while Batch B has 100 days remaining but only US approval. When US demand arrives requiring minimum shelf life of  80 days, the system automatically routes Batch B to this demand - all within the standard planning run.

No spreadsheet exports. No manual approval checking. No custom code calculating time decay.

The technical challenge this solves is significant: as you add characteristics (regulatory approvals, quality grades) with Shelf life, the combinatorial complexity explodes. Planning must evaluate every supply against every demand requirement while time continues to erode shelf life. The system handles all constraints natively, during your regular planning cycles.

Other Benefits

  • Enhanced Shelf-Life Planning capabilities
  • Ability to use CBP and Enhanced Shelf-Life planning independently or together!
  • For, SAP7F, enable the enhanced function by setting a global configuration parameter 
  • For HPA, only the new enhanced Shelf-Life solution will be available – No special parameter for activation

Enhanced Shelf-Life Planning capabilities include:

  • improved precision with Lot Sizing: Old Min/Max building versus new lot grouping id as used for CBP
  • Shelf-Life Propagation to components and inheritance: Finished product’s usable life is constrained by the remaining Shelf-Life of a critical lower-level BoM component (currently restricted to one component)
  • there's more to come in subsequent releases

Business Outcomes:

Executives can finally eliminate the waste-versus-compliance tradeoff. The organization reduces spoilage while maintaining its compliance posture. Supply chain leaders reclaim planner productivity. Teams stop spending hours manually routing batches and start focusing on exception management and strategic decisions. IT organizations operate using standard SAP capabilities including integration to ERP --> TCO drop potential.

Whats Next: 

Shelf life planning with regulatory compliance is available now in SAP IBP 2602. Watch our demo video to it in action, or connect with your SAP team to assess fit for your organization.

Video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tFybO70a0Ls 

This release aligns into our harmonized ways for planning and paves the way forward for subsequent releases where we build on this for some complex use cases. Stay tuned!

This is the third blog in the series: First two blogs can be found here: 

Unlock the Potential of Characteristics-Based Planning (CBP) in SAP IBP

Breaking the Silos:Orchestrate end to end Characteristics Based Plans across Planning and Execution

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

entry.title: Re: PR Re-Approval After Deletion
entry.content:
Hello@Keratiya_D

Question: Can you lock an approved PR so it cannot be change once a PO exists?

The public cloud does not provide a standard way to do that. That means it is not supported to generally restrict an approved PR and because a follow-on document exists. It is a limitation, see KBA 3498391 for more details. So, you also cannot enforce the rule “PO item must be deleted first, then PR can be changed” as a standard system control.

Instead of that you may consider

  1. Making changes go through re approval. If your main concern is governance related, I suggest you use PR workflow “restart on change” setting so that any relevant change after approval triggers a new approval cycle. This does not lock the PR but it ensures you have control over changes.
  2. If your goal is to stop further follow on actions, you can close or set deletion indicator on the PR item (depending on your process) but this still does not equal a hard lock for all fields.
  3. Custom validation that checks follow on PO exists and raises an error on change will be an option if you must really block edits when a PO exist. This is what I know will enforce POs from being edited unless it is deleted.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Confirming quantities with a handheld device in SAP H...
entry.content:
Hello @AngelT 

Thank you for the details. 

Your description reflects a consistent behaviour. In Post Goods Receipt for Purchasing Document, the GTIN column is typically the main GTIN of the product, not automatically the GTIN of each alternative UoM. So, if your product master has only one main GTIN, both PO items will display the same GTIN, even if the items use different order units.

If you want the app to show different GTINs per unit, you must maintain GTIN per UoM and set a main GTIN per UoM. See  Creating Products and  Creating Products for Retail for more details.

  • Consider Maintaining GTIN per UoM in the product master.

Open Manage Product Master Data, 

Open Material, go to UoM, for each alternative UoM; maintain the correct GTIN and ensure there is a Main GTIN for that UoM (not only for the base unit).

This is required if you scanning is to automatically identify the right presentation.

  • Thereafter, you need to ensure the PO item order unit matches your intended GTIN UoM. Otherwise, the system falls back to the product main GTIN and both lines look identical.
  • You can then retest in the GR app. In Post GR for Purchasing Document, enter the PO, use the scan button (You have “Escanear” in your screenshot), scan the GTIN. If you maintain GTIN per UoM correctly, the app can match the scanned GTIN to the right line and update the quantity. 

Should you still get the same GTIN after the first step described above, then the GTIN field shown is still reading only the product’s overall main GTIN. In that case, I suggest the following workaround:

  1. Use separate material per presentation, each with its own GTIN, or
  2. Use a warehouse scanning process based Handling Units.

 

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Easy Cooking, Part II: When AI Finally Served the Full ABAP Code
entry.content:
Three years ago I published a post with a suspiciously delicious title: “Easy Cooking: SM58 LUW_extractor (Extract pdf from logs in SM58 transaction)”. This is the sequel — the second serving — where the same SM58 problem returns to the kitchen, but this time the sous-chef is AI… and it finally knows how to finish the recipe.

       Back in 2023, when I first wrote “Easy Cooking: SM58 LUW_extractor (Extract pdf from logs in SM58 transaction)”, I had a very simple plan: get the PDF out of SM58, no matter what it takes. And by “what it takes,” I obviously mean: write it in C#, because my ABAP skill level then (and now) is still proudly parked at “Hello World”. 😄

So yes — I cooked the whole thing in C# first. Not because it was the “correct” SAP-native choice, but because it was the choice that allowed me to actually finish the meal without setting the kitchen on fire. The extractor worked, the PDFs came out, everyone was happy… and my ABAP editor remained untouched and peacefully unused, like a gym membership in January.

Then came the “side quest”: Could this tool live outside of .NET?
With a bit of help from AI, I managed to port the C# logic into PHP. That part was surprisingly smooth — like swapping pasta brands and still ending up with dinner. Different syntax, same recipe, same result.

But ABAP? Oh, ABAP was the boss fight.

At the time, I tried multiple times to translate the same logic into ABAP, again with AI assistance. The result was always the same: I’d get something that looked like ABAP, smelled like ABAP… and then dumped like a dramatic opera singer the moment I pressed “Activate.” The best I could get was a collection of promising fragments — a method here, a structure there, a confident comment like “this should work” — and then I’d spend the rest of the day proving that it did not, in fact, work.

So I did what any reasonable developer would do: I left it alone and pretended it was “not a priority.” 

Fast forward ~3 years…

One day, the thought returned: What if I tried again?
Same problem. Same repository. Same C# code sitting on GitHub, looking at me like: “Remember when you said you’d rewrite me in ABAP?”

This time, I decided to make it a proper challenge. I gave AI the GitHub URL and basically said:

“Here’s the C# implementation. Please rewrite it in ABAP.”

And I expected the usual: a motivational speech, a half-solution, and a polite suggestion that I should “consider using standard SAP functionality.”

Instead… AI just went to the repo, read the C# code, and came back like a calm senior developer who had already fixed the issue while I was still explaining it.

It generated the complete ABAP program — not just snippets, not just pseudo-code — an actual, end-to-end translation of the logic. The kind of output that makes you blink twice and check if you accidentally asked a coworker instead of a chatbot.

Of course, I didn’t trust it immediately. (I’ve been hurt before.)
So I did what we all do when something looks too good to be true: I tested it like a suspicious QA engineer with free time and a personal vendetta.

And here’s the plot twist:

It worked… after a few iterations.
Not “first try, perfection, angels singing.”
More like: “Nice attempt… now let’s fix this dump, and that edge case, and the one thing that looked innocent but absolutely wasn’t.”

So yes — there were a couple of back-and-forth rounds with AI and some testing on my side. But the important part is: the core solution was real, the logic was there, and with a bit of iterative seasoning, the ABAP version became fully functional.

The PDF extraction ran cleanly, the output made sense, and the program behaved like it had been written by someone who actually enjoys ABAP (which is definitely not my “Hello World” self).

So this Part II is really the story of that moment:
the same recipe, the same ingredients — but suddenly the kitchen gained a chef.

 Figure 1. Shows how rewritten code in ABAP extracts and opens PDF from SM58 LUW's log

Summary

That’s it for Part II of this “Easy Cooking” experiment. If you’ve tried extracting PDFs from SM58 logs (or fought with LUWs in general), I’d love to hear how you approached it — and whether you’d do anything differently. Drop a comment with your thoughts, improvements, or war stories.

The ABAP version (ZSM58_LUW_EXTRACT_ABAP) is available in the GitHub repository, and the AI model used for the rewrite was ChatGPT 5.2. Feel free to test it, break it, improve it — and if you spot a better way, share it with the rest of us.

NOTE:

Bonus idea for Part III (because sequels always happen): instead of the current “save the log → upload → extract” routine, we could make it even more lazy-developer-friendly. Imagine a mode where you just paste the SM58 Transaction ID, hit execute, and the ABAP does the rest — one input field, one click, one PDF… and zero file juggling.

entry.title: Re: S/4HANA Public Cloud: How to restrict Credit Release...
entry.content:
Good day @KatjaWa 

Thank you for your questions.

You can do that but it is not maintained on the DCD itself. The recheck job skips your document because the release validity period is still open, so the system does not re run the credit check for already released documents. See https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/c45b25e805db4350ba433b01dfe3c6e0.html for details.

For you to make manual release valid for same day only, you need to set the validity period for release to zero for the relevant recheck logic. With that, the release remains valid for the release day and on the next calendar day the release date is no longer within the validity window, so the recheck run will include the document again. See https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/c45b25e805db4350ba433b01dfe3c6e0.html for more insights.

You should maintain this in your credit management rule configuration, not in MIGO or OVA8

  • Note that if your job still skips the document, check that you are changing the same rule that is by the document’s credit segment and check step.
  • Also ensure your tenant time zone and date change timing since your same day requirement is calendar based.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: 面向 SAP ERP 公有云、SAP IBP、SAP Marketing Cloud 用户的提醒：出站“默认客户端证书”CA 将变更为 SAP Cloud Root CA
entry.content:
背景与原因

  • 受行业规范变化（由 Google 推动）影响，主流证书颁发机构将停止签发用于客户端认证的证书。当前为 SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition、SAP IBP、SAP Marketing Cloud 租户签发“默认客户端证书”的 DigiCert  也将停止相关签发。
  • 为确保持续可用，SAP 将把“默认客户端证书”的签发 CA 切换为 SAP Cloud Root CA 。自 2026 年 3 月起，新签发的客户端证书将由 SAP Cloud Root CA 颁发，并在后续的常规证书轮换中逐步替换现有证书。

谁需要关注与行动

  • 如果您在 S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition、IBP 或 SAP Marketing Cloud 的出站集成中使用“默认客户端证书”进行认证（即由您的租户调用目标系统），本次变更适用且需要您在目标系统执行调整。
  • 仅涉及客户自管的集成场景；SAP 管理的场景由 SAP 处理。

本次变更的具体内容

  • 证书“issuer（颁发者）”从 DigiCert Global G2 TLS RSA SHA256 2020 CA1 变更为 SAP Cloud Root CA。
  • 证书“subject（主题）”将新增 OU 字段，OU 的值为签发该证书的 BTP Certificate Service 的子账户 ID。
  • 证书链相应更换为 SAP Cloud Root CA 体系。部分场景中证书扩展用途可能调整为仅客户端认证（Client Authentication）。

时间线与通知方式

  • 自 2026 年 3 月下旬起，新开系统将直接获得由 SAP Cloud Root CA 签发的默认客户端证书；存量系统将按既有到期轮换节奏完成切换（不进行“集中大迁移”）。部分租户可能在 2 月下旬进入证书准备或分阶段发布过程。
  • 您现有的证书在其到期日前仍然有效；切换发生在下一次证书轮换时。示例：若当前证书有效期至 2026 年 4 月，则变更在 4 月轮换时生效；若至 11 月，则 11 月再切换。
  • 与以往一致，SAP 将通过邮件通知证书轮换；您也可在源租户的 Maintain Client Certificates 应用查看证书有效期与使用处。

如果不更新，会有什么影响？

若不更新目标系统配置，可能出现两类问题：

  • CA 信任失败：目标系统未信任 SAP Cloud Root CA，mTLS 握手在 TLS 层直接失败（通常无 HTTP 状态码）。
  • 身份映射错误：目标系统依赖证书主题/颁发者做用户或系统身份映射，未更新映射会导致 401/403 等授权错误，或因实现差异出现 TLS 错误。

您需要做什么（行动清单）

       1. 在目标系统更新 CA 信任

  • 将 SAP Cloud Root CA 的根证书导入目标系统的信任库（trust store）。可从 SAP Trust Center 获取 SAP Cloud Root CA 根证书。

       2. 切换到新的“默认客户端证书”

  • 参考源租户的 Maintain Client Certificates 应用，在证书轮换发生后，将出站通信用户/连接凭据从旧证书切换到新证书（与以往轮换操作一致）。
  • 若目标为 BTP 服务实例，请在对应服务绑定/服务密钥中引用新证书；最简做法是基于新证书重建绑定/密钥。原“用户映射”在 BTP 中表现为“系统映射”，同样需更新。

       3. 更新身份映射逻辑

  • 针对目标系统中基于证书 subject/issuer 的用户或系统映射，调整为使用新证书的元数据（包含新的 OU 与新的 issuer）。

       4. 使用证书钉扎（pinning）的环境

  • 若目标系统采用证书指纹钉扎，不涉及 CA 信任；在轮换时直接替换为新证书指纹即可。但这类环境每次证书轮换都需更新指纹。

如何定位“所有受影响的目标系统”

  • 在源租户打开 维护客户端证书（Maintain Client Certificates） 应用。
  • 选择 SAP 管理的“客户端缺省值（Client Default）”证书。
  • 在“通信系统”区域查看该证书被使用的所有通信系统列表。
  • 点击进入具体通信系统，在“出站通信的用户（Users for Outbound Communication）”中查看认证方式：
    • 若为“SSL客户端证书（SSL Certificate）”，在“常规（General）”下查看目标系统主机并在该系统完成信任与映射更新。
    • 若为“OAuth 2.0（mTLS）”，该证书用于向 OAuth 令牌提供者的 mTLS 端点认证，应在令牌提供者侧更新；端点位置可在“OAuth 2.0 Settings -> Outbound OAuth 2.0 Client Settings”查看。

常见问答与提示

  • 是否会集中切换？不会。切换随各租户证书到期的常规轮换进行。
  • 旧证书是否马上失效？不会，旧证书在到期前有效。
  • 新根证书来源？从 SAP Trust Center 下载 SAP Cloud Root CA 根证书。
  • SAP 会发邮件吗？会，轮换通知邮件将包含 CA 变更说明；请按指引在目标系统完成信任与映射更新。

支持与文档资源

您可以阅读以下文档，获取更多详细信息：

  • SAP Note 3677763 - Change of Client Certificate "Client Default" in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP IBP, and SAP Marketing Cloud - SAP for Me
  • Don't Let Your Integration Take a Coffee Break: Cl... - SAP Community
  • How to Handle Default Client Certificate Renewal
  • Set Up Certificate-to-User Mapping (SAP Cloud Integration and SAP Marketing Cloud)
  • Client Certificate Authentication for Integration Flow Processing (SAP Integration Suite)
  • Client Certificate Authentication for API Clients (SAP Integration Suite)
  • 如遇问题，请按产品提交事件：XX-S4C-OPR-INC（S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition）、SCM-IBP-OPS-INC（SAP IBP）、CEC-MKT-ITC（SAP Marketing Cloud）

结语

对使用“默认客户端证书”的客户而言，本次 CA 变更是一次必要的合规升级。只要您在目标系统及时导入 SAP Cloud Root CA 根证书、切换到新客户端证书并更新身份映射，出站集成即可平稳度过“轮换窗口”，避免握手失败或授权错误。

entry.title: Minimum CU for Datasphere with BDC Connector?
entry.content:
Hello Community,

We are currently evaluating the architecture for performing zero-copy data sharing from SAP Data Products to Databricks using the SAP BDC Connector.

According to my understanding, when using the BDC Connector for zero-copy data sharing, an SAP Datasphere tenant is still required. I would like to confirm whether the minimum Capacity Units (CU) required for Datasphere in this scenario follows the same logic described in the following blog post, which explains the minimum CU needed to activate the Object Store:

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/what-is-the-minimum-capacity-units-cu-to-activate-object-store-in-sap/ba-p/14181475

When performing zero-copy sharing of SAP Data Products to Databricks via the SAP BDC Connector, is the minimum CU requirement for Datasphere simply the minimum CU needed to activate the Object Store, as described in the blog above?

Thank you!

entry.title: Missing Productkey
entry.content:
We PERSON_NAME a productkey and need a new one.

Question and Answer:

entry.title: Re: Return to Supplier in SAP S4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
@AngelT if PO has one item, you want to return this item with full quantity, please refer to "Movement type 122 with the reason for return movement specified referencing original material document.".

If you PO has multiple items and you want to return a sub number of them, please refer to 'Material document with movement type 161 (Returns for purchase order)'

entry.title: Re: Unable to raise incidents on installation number xxxx
entry.content:
Hi customer,

Normally if an on-premise technical installation is missing for case creating or remote connection, it is because of the following possible reasons:

  • S-user does not have the relevant authorizations on the missing installation
  • Hardware defined in the installation is expired (e.g. Operating System and Database has a "valid to" date that expired) or there is no hardware data defined, or the maintenance frame is expired on the installation
  • Installation number has been deleted
  • There is no any active system under the installation number 

All the reasons and trouble-shootings are documented in KBA 1850002 - Installation number missing in System Data, SSCR, License Keys, or when reporting a case - SAP for Me.

Kindly check the installation status in Manage Installation app also the S-user authorization on your side first.

If everything is fine with your installation, the installation is active with valid maintenance and hardware and active system(s) under it, but still it's missing for case creation, feel free to create a case using component XX-SER-FORME with the issue details. Just select another active system to report the case.

Hope the above information helps.

Thanks and regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: PRINT with JDBC connection
entry.content: What JDBC driver are you using. The PRINT statement [T-SQL]  returns a message to the client window if you are connected from an Open Client application or jConnect application. If you are connected from an Embedded SQL or ODBC application, the message is displayed in the database server messages window. The SQL Anywhere JDBC Driver provides a JDBC Callback for handling the MESSAGE statement.

entry.title: Re: BKP: AI-Assisted Input Assistance in the “Manage Customer Returns–Version 2” App
entry.content:
Hi @Jwan_24, 

DAR is already set for deprecation and price list removal will be effective from 31st March 2026. Generally, we would not recommend to set up the DAR service at this point. If you have specific reasons e.g. needing to deliver a PoC urgently, please do reach out to me with your use case and we can review it on a case-by-case basis.

The current guidance is to use SAP-RPT-1 in place of DAR. SAP-RPT-1 is already available on SAP generative AI hub, so you can already try it out and evaluate the model performance on your use case / dataset. 

Hope this helps!

Regards,
Sherene

entry.title: SAP Commerce Cloud Open Payment Framework -Lower Costs, Quicker Deployments and Greater Flexibility
entry.content:
Overview

Integrating SAP Commerce Cloud with a payment service provider can be challenging for several reasons. The integration is highly proprietary and requires custom code, which results in higher development and maintenance costs. It may take longer to integrate a new payment service provider or expand into new regions. Open Payment Framework (OPF) is developed and released to address the challenges.

Let’s look at the Key challenges and pain points for the ecommerce business in payments perspective 

Key challenges and pain points associated with traditional payment service provider implementations: effectively addressed by the Open Payment Framework.

  • Integration Complexity:  Traditional payment integration requires individual configuration points between various commerce and payment service providers, it adds custom development burden (need to code, integrate, and deploy extensions to the Commerce Cloud codebase)
  • Scalability: Implementation restricted to specific payment providers due to custom code, making it challenging and costly to switch between PSPs (Each implementation costs months to develop, test and deploy)
  • PCI Compliance:  Every PSP implementation needs to maintain PCI DSS compliance also need to eliminate the risk of storing critical credit card data
  • Limitations: Limitations of being restricted to only credit card payments, enabling support for alternative payment methods like PayPal, Alipay, and Buy Now Pay Later may need additional options and effort
  • Regional Payment Constraints:  Retailers need to pick payment partners based on unique requirements across various markets and regions

Open Payment Framework (OPF) – What Is It?

The Open Payment Framework (OPF) is a cloud-based service within SAP Commerce Cloud that provides a centralized, plug-and-play solution for integrating digital payment service providers. Through this central hub, SAP Commerce Cloud and payment service providers (PSPs) can communicate more easily and efficiently, eliminating custom coding and complex integrations that can be expensive to build and maintain.

  • SAP Commerce Cloud's open payment framework allows you to integrate your preferred digital Payment Service Provider (PSP) intuitively
  • It is available for all SAP Commerce cloud customers as part of existing contract without any additional cost.
  • Enables BYOG (Bring your own gateway) with low Code /No Code integration with Payment Service Providers
  • Provides prebuilt Accelerators serving as reference templates for payment gateways like Adyen, Stripe, Worldpay, Airwallex, Checkout.com and many more across regions
  • Delivers a Composable Storefront (Spartacus) Extension to enable Merchants to quickly integrate Payment UIs (also provide service support for accelerator-based storefront, third party JS storefront)  
  • This platform offers a single, consolidated view of payment transactions from all service providers, along with problem diagnostic tooling like Request and Notification Logs
  • OPF can coexist with existing legacy payment integrations which are implemented already
  • PSP configuration effort - Using existing sample template, it will be less taking hours and PSP configuration from scratch (new payment gateway which is not onboarded in OPF) will take 1-3 days

 

Business Benefits of Open Payment Framework (OPF) implementation

  • No Additional Cost: Available for all SAP Commerce cloud customers as part of existing contract without any additional cost
  • Accelerated Integration: Prebuilt, standardized connections eliminate custom integrations, significantly reducing deployment time for new payment methods and providers
  • Broder Coverage: Support for payment providers and support to alternate payment methods across regions
  • Centralized Management: Provides unified payment integration, logging, and management across all payment service providers in a single interface
  • Automated Reconciliation: Eliminates manual effort in post-processing bank statements through automated reconciliation of payments with open items
  • Reduced Development and operation Costs: Low-code/no-code interface minimizes development resources required for payment integration
  • Enhanced Customer Experience: Provides customers with diverse digital payment options while ensuring smooth end-to-end payment experiences
  • Market Agility: Enables businesses to adapt quickly to changing market dynamics and customer preferences through flexible payment provider switching
  • Enhanced Security: Tokenization approach ensures PCI compliance while maintaining secure payment processing - PCI DSS 4.0 AOC certification.
  • Comprehensive Transaction Support: Supports all traditional payment methods including payment authorization, capture, refund, cancellation, and provides unified transaction visibility  
  • Flexible UX Patterns: Supports multiple payment form patterns including hosted pages, iframe integration, and hosted fields for maximum flexibility

Storefronts communicate with the open payment framework (OPF) via REST Web services.

OPF processes the requests from the consumer applications and routes them to the relevant external payment service provider (PSP). It contains one adapter for connecting the open payment framework with SAP Commerce Cloud. The open payment framework core service and the open payment framework workbench enable the connection to external PSPs.

The open payment framework and the external PSPs communicate using Web services. Most of this communication is synchronous.

SAP Commerce Cloud - Open payment framework usage guidance

OPF can be implemented for new / existing Commerce Cloud (2211.35 and above) customers. Existing customers may use OPF to add new payment providers while retaining the existing legacy implementation or they can migrate entirely to OPF as single solution

OPF supports SAP Commerce Composable Storefront with out of the box also support the accelerator storefront with sample code, it also supports third party storefront using OPF JS library  

Resources to refer

  • Early Adopter Care Registration
  • Training- OPF Microlearning Videos
  • Onboarding guide
  • Help center documentation
  • Demo Video - SAP Commerce Cloud, open payment framework Overview - SAP Media Share
  • Commerce Cloud Services Detail for OPF

Call-to-Action Suggestion

Explore how your organization can leverage SAP Commerce Cloud Open Payment Framework to accelerate payment innovation and reduce integration complexity in your digital commerce strategy. Please contact your SAP representative for more information 

Thanks much for your time, hope the information is helpful. All The best!
